course_title: Psychosocial Aspects of Occupational Therapy Practice

learning_outcomes.description: Act in a professional manner that aligns with the standards and values of occupational therapy profession, including maintenance of professional requirements.
Identify and apply the frames of reference and theories relevant to psychosocial practice in occupational therapy.
Identify and analyse the interdependence between physical, psychosocial and mental health fields of practice.

Identify the importance of the roles played by significant others (e.g. family members) and explain the potential impact of the client's behavioural, social and emotional challenges on significant others.
Analyse and evaluate the factors that contribute to the development and maintenance of a range of behavioural, social and emotional challenges in adulthood.
Identify and use a range of formal and informal information gathering approaches used in the fields of psychosocial practice.
Apply and evaluate appropriate approaches to improving occupational performance and participation of individuals and families where there are social-emotional and/or behavioural challenges.

Evaluate the best available evidence to generate clinical decisions when working with people with social, emotional, and/or behavioural challenges, those with mental health issues, and those experiencing challenging transitions.

Use and carry-out a range of professional and effective communication skills in developing and maintaining therapeutic and professional relationships, including ability to effectively work with others.
Reflect on and provide critical appraisal of own and others performance as required and implement appropriate changes in response to this appraisal.
Recognise and reflect on interprofessional practice skills including role clarification, team functioning, interprofessional communication, and client-centred care.

assessments.due_date:
31/07/2026 4:00 pm

1 week after your in-person tutorial. This equates to 31 July (TUT1, TUT2), 7 August (TUT3) or 11 September (TUT4).
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Essay/ Critique, Presentation, Project
assessments.task_description:
The assessment will be completed in your timetabled tutorial class, with a final submission date set a week later.

This is a team assessment, and all team members are expected to contribute equally to the team’s assessment items during the tutorial. Teams will be assigned prior to the tutorial. There are two (2) parts to this assessment – a worksheet based upon a client video that your team will choose during the tutorial class, and a team check in with your tutor.

Part One

Part One requires you to complete a worksheet and will draw on the content of the GEMS IPCP module one, focusing on role-clarification and negotiation, team communication, team functioning, and relationship focused care and services

Length: There is no word limit for the worksheet.

Part Two

Part Two involves a check-in with a tutor, to assess and reflect on processes of collaboration.

Length: approx. 10 minutes

Teams will need to submit Part One in the Assessment tab on the IPCP Learn.UQ course site at the end of the tutorial class. Additional information regarding submission will be provided on the HABS1240 GEMS IPCP Learn.UQ course site. 

Please refer to the assessment information on Learn.UQ for further details and requirements for the assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Students are required to write an occupational formulation report, focusing on the person's psychosocial health and well-being. This is an individual assessment and not a must pass assessment. Further information is available on Learn.UQ.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and or Machine Translation (MT) tools

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

Use of AI/MT tools and copyright

Students are reminded that the video case study provided for this assignment has been designed and developed by the University of Queensland. Uploading any part of this case study, including transcripts, to external platforms, AI tools, or MT tools may constitute a breach of copyright. Please ensure that you use the materials responsibly and in accordance with the University's academic integrity.

assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

Alternative Exam Arrangements can be made (and applied to in-semester exams) in specific circumstances. Please see information on circumstances and how to apply at Alternative Exam Arrangements (https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/alternative-exam-arrangements). Exam adjustments are separate to a Student Access Plan (Disability). Your SAP does NOT apply to exams. This only covers in-class participation, activities, and assessment. If you have approved Alternative Exam Arrangements and you have a course with school-based examinations (e.g. mid-semester exams, in-class quizzes, online quizzes, tutorial exercises etc.) then please advise the course coordinator and examinations of the adjustments and assessments respectively as soon as possible to increase the potential for arrangements to be accommodated.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment occurs as scheduled in the ECP. Students will be required to watch a video and complete a quiz to demonstrate their foundational knowledge of Mental State Examination and their ability to apply the knowledge to a case. Further information will be provided on Learn UQ. In the event of a disruption that prevents the scheduled assessment from occurring as planned, the assessment will be changed to an alternative form of assessment, such as a Blackboard exam. The timing of the assessment may also be impacted.

Students are expected to check connectivity prior to the online quiz to ensure they can complete this assessment. If they experience issues with connection, they will be required to submit evidence (such as screenshot). These affected students with appropriate evidence, then, may be given another opportunity to complete this quiz.

This is not a must-pass assessment.

Use of AI and MT tools

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of the content independent of AI and MT tools.

Use of AI/MT tools and Copyright

Students are reminded that third-party materials provided for this assessment, including videos, transcripts, and other media, are subject to copyright. These materials may have been developed by external organisations or institutions, including the University of Queensland.

Uploading any part of these materials to external platforms, artificial intelligence (AI) tools, or machine translation (MT) tools may constitute a breach of copyright. Students must use all provided content responsibly and in accordance with the University's academic integrity and copyright policies.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The students will be required to access this online quiz via Learn.UQ. Please refer to the task description on Learn.UQ, including the time limit applied to this online quiz (exam).

assessments.late_submission: Exams submitted after the end of the submission time will incur a late penalty.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.due_date: Block 1 (Monday Week 7 at 10 am), Block 2 (Monday Week 12 at 10 am)
assessments.task_description:
This assessment is related to sessional placement. Students are to reflect on the role of their clinical educator in mental health clinical practice. See the rubric and task description on Learn.UQ for more detail. The due date depends on the timing of the placement. Use Turnitin to submit this assignment.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and or Machine Translation (MT) tools

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

Use of Generative AI/MT in Student Placements

Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Large Language Models (e.g., ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot) are increasingly used in society. In terms of clinical placements, students are not permitted to use AI/MT unless explicitly indicated by the provider that this use is permitted, and the parameters of use are clear. If these are not explicit, students are expected to consult with their practice educators before implementation to determine the acceptability of generative AI/MT use and ensure that any potential risks associated with the intended use are addressed. AI is not a replacement of, or substitute for, professional reasoning or clinical skills. Human oversight is essential in its use, and to employ generative AI effectively and responsibly, students must understand how AI works, including its functions, data storage methods, and its impact on adherence to professional codes of conduct.

If used on placement, students are responsible for any outputs generated by AI, such as clinical notes and reports, and will be held accountable for the actions of any AI/MT that is used in clinical practice. This responsibility includes ensuring adherence to the relevant profession's code of conduct and ethical standards. Transparency about generative AI/MT use is essential in placement environments. Students must follow the relevant policies and procedures of their placement provider. Students are reminded that they need to demonstrate their ability to meet the inherent requirements of the relevant profession, operate within its scope of practice, and ensure that client/patient privacy and confidentiality are maintained at all times. Under no circumstances should identifiable clinical information be uploaded to open or external AI/MT platforms, as it is unethical and irresponsible.

assessments.task_description:
Details of the exam format will be provided on LearnUQ. The examination will cover the content of all lectures, tutorials and readings. The exam also covers the content that is introduced in the placement tutorial sessions. This exam will include case-based short answer responses to questions.

Exam will be conducted via Inspera online (on campus).

In the event of disruption during the end of semester exam period that prevents the schedule assessment occurring as planned, the assessment will be changed to an alternative form of assessments, such as a Blackboard exam. The timing of the assessment may also be impacted. This is a must-pass assessment. Please note that the minimum pass mark for this asessment is 55%.

Use of AI/MT Tools

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

learning_activities.topic:
Interprofessional Collaborative Practice(IPCP)

You will work in small teams in the tutorial with students from: Audiology, Dentistry, Dietetics, Nursing, Occupational Therapy, Physiotherapy, Psychology, Social Work and Speech Pathology. There is one module consisting of on-line preparation and a face-to-face tutorial class. You will work in your small team to engage in a range of learning activities to develop your knowledge and skills to help prepare you for working in interprofessional collaborative teams when caring for clients. 

1 x Tutorial – 3 hours

You will need to attend one of the following sessions: Friday 24th July 9am or 1pm, Friday 31st July 9am or Friday 4th September 9am. You will be scheduled to attend one of the sessions.


Learning outcomes: L01, L09, L10, L11

learning_activities.topic:
Weekly pre-recorded lecture

There is one two-hour of pre-recorded lecture per week. The recordings will be made available on Learn.UQ. Students are expected to independently manage workload so they can watch weekly lecture recordings before attending tutorial sessions.

The detailed lecture schedule is available on Learn.UQ.

Use of AI/MT and copyright

Students are reminded that lecture notes and recordings provided in this course have been developed by the University of Queensland and/or third-party contributors. Uploading any part of these materials, including transcripts or audio content, to external platforms, artificial intelligence (AI) tools, or machine translation (MT) tools may constitute a breach of copyright.

Please ensure that you use all course materials responsibly and in accordance with the University's academic integrity and copyright policies.

learning_activities.topic:
Weekly tutorial

These weekly 2-hour tutorials will extend the content of lectures, provide additional content, and increase practical engagement. During tutorials, students are expected to apply the knowledge introduced in relevant weekly lecture. Tutorials will be delivered as a face-to-face session. Due to the interactive nature, tutorials will not be recorded. However, due to the unforeseen circumstances, the delivery mode may be changed to online. Please note that active participation within the tutorials is essential for the successful completion of ALL assessment items.

The detailed tutorial schedule is available on Learn.UQ.

Use of AI/MT and copyright

Students are reminded that tutorial notes and other contents provided in this course have been developed by the University of Queensland and/or third-party contributors. Uploading any part of these materials to external platforms, artificial intelligence (AI) tools, or machine translation (MT) tools may constitute a breach of copyright.

Please ensure that you use all course materials responsibly and in accordance with the University's academic integrity and copyright policies.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08, L09

learning_activities.topic:
Placement Orientation

This is a one-off mandatory placement orientation. This orientation is a face-to-face session. Further information will be provided on Learn.UQ. Both Block 1 and 2 students to attend this session. This placement orientation counts towards the 1000 placement hours required by the World Federation of Occupational Therapists to be accumulated during your degree before graduation.

Absentee Process

If you are unable to attend for a legitimate reason (i.e., medical or extenuating circumstances), you are required to email a completed absentee form (available on Learn.UQ) to the School of Health and Rehabilitation Sciences Student and Academic Support Unit (enquiries.shrs@uq.edu.au). The absentee form to complete is located in Blackboard/Learning Resources/Course Information Folder. Please remember to cc your Course Coordinator and Clinical Educator into the email and attach your required evidence. The Student and Academic Support Unit will respond to you via email outlining whether you have been approved, and they will inform course staff of the outcome. With approval, you will be required to complete make-up activities by the due date.

Failure to communicate one's absence in advance without suitable medical or extenuating circumstances will result in not meeting the requirements of this course.


Learning outcomes: L01, L09, L10

learning_activities.topic:
Sessional placement tutorial session

This is a tutorial that is attached to the sessional placement and can be used as a weekly debriefing session while being on placement. Details will be provided on Learn.UQ and during the orientation session in week one. The contents from these tutorials are examinable. While students are on placement, they are expected to attend this placement tutorial (i.e., Block 1 students to attend this tutorial during Block 1 placement).

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 2 To Week 11(03 Aug - 18 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Sessional placement

All students are required to participate in this sessional placement. This placement counts towards the 1000 placement hours required by the World Federation of Occupational Therapists to be accumulated during your degree. Due to the nature of health and disability service restrictions and requirements, this planned learning activity may be modified, for example on-line case-based learning experiences or simulation-based learning experiences on UQ campus. Students are to submit signed attendance records for the mental health clinical placement component. Please note that unless students meet the placement compliance requirements, they will not be able to commence their placement.

Placement attendance

Students are expected to attend all scheduled placement sessions.

Absences may be approved under specific circumstances, including:

  • Medical grounds
  • Exceptional circumstances
  • Student Access Plan

Absences will not be approved where the School determines that reasonable steps were not taken to avoid the circumstances. Examples of non-approved reasons include:

  • Holiday arrangements
  • Misreading the time or date
  • Social or leisure events
  • Work commitments

Students should refer to the School’s Absentee Form for a full list of non-permissible circumstances and further guidance.

Absentee Process

If you are unable to attend for a legitimate reason (i.e., medical or extenuating circumstances), you are required to email a completed absentee form (available on Learn.UQ) to the School of Health and Rehabilitation Sciences Student and Academic Support Unit (enquiries.shrs@uq.edu.au). The absentee form to complete is located in Blackboard/Learning Resources/Course Information Folder. Please remember to cc your Clinical Educator into the email and attach your required evidence. The Student and Academic Support Unit will respond to you via email outlining whether you have been approved, and they will inform course staff of the outcome. Failure to communicate one's absence in advance without suitable medical or extenuating circumstances will result in not meeting the requirements of this course.


Learning outcomes: L01, L06, L09, L10
